title: Lime begins life as a public company after years of uncertainty
slides: Can a dockless scooter company finally turn a profit?
Lime quietly filed for an IPO with positive unit economics.
Despite 95% of rides being under 15 minutes per trip.
You now get route-based pricing, not just per-minute rates.
Test the new fare in your city before your next competitor does.
thread_text:
1. Is profit finally here?

Lime filed for IPO confidentially, claiming positive unit economics for the first time.

2. Short rides, big problem.

95% of trips are under 15 minutes, making per-minute pricing a losing bet.

3. The math changed.

Lime now charges based on route, not just time — raising average revenue per ride.

4. Test before copy.

This route-based pricing is a live experiment, not a final product.

5. Can you beat them?

Run the new fare model in your city this week, before your next competitor does.
video_script: Now they charge by route, not time.
Test it in your city first.

script: Now they charge by route, not time.
Test it in your city first.

instagram.caption:
95% of Lime rides are under 15 minutes — but now you’ll pay based on the route, not just the clock.

Lime quietly filed for an IPO with positive unit economics for the first time. The old per-minute model was a losing bet. The new pricing raises revenue per trip by charging for the distance and path, not the time.

For you: your city's pricing is a live experiment, not a final model.

Can you test this new fare in your city before your next competitor does this week? 🛴💸

carousel_slides.description: Lime filed for IPO confidentially, claiming positive unit economics for the first time.
95% of rides are under 15 minutes, making per-minute pricing a losing strategy.
Now charging by route instead of time, boosting average revenue per ride significantly.
Route-based pricing is a live experiment, not final. Run it in your city this week.
Save this post, then test Lime's new fare model in your market before rivals copy it.
